note: Qualitative capability matrix, not an n=30 distribution. jj's behaviour here is deterministic for a pinned 0.43.x: the bisect outcome and the compiler-of-history search reproduce on a re-run. The one thing that does NOT reproduce byte-for-byte is the change-id / git-hash strings, which jj randomizes per repo — see manifest.determinism. Every 'observed' below is recorded firsthand from the 2026-07-15 run.
subject: Jujutsu (jj) 0.43.0 — automated bisect + revision-tree search on a git-colocated repo. See manifest.json for the full environment.

support_matrix.case: jj bisect run auto-narrows an 8-commit linear history to the commit that planted a mean() off-by-one
support_matrix.command: jj bisect run --range 'base..@' -- python -B -c "import stats,sys; sys.exit(0 if stats.mean([2.0,4.0,6.0])==4.0 else 1)"
support_matrix.expectation: reports the planted commit as first-bad
support_matrix.observed: first bad revision = 'refactor: tweak mean() internals' (the planted commit), reached in 3 evaluations of 6 candidates
support_matrix.mechanism: bisect binary-searches the range: head (@) is assumed bad, the excluded ancestor (base) good; each step checks out a midpoint, runs the test, and halves the remaining range. The test is a pure function of the tree, so the search is monotone and lands exactly on the commit that changed mean().

support_matrix.case: the test's exit code tells bisect good / bad / skip / abort
support_matrix.observed: 0 = good, non-zero (except below) = bad, 125 = skip (untestable), 127 = abort. $JJ_BISECT_TARGET carries the commit id under test; --find-good reverses the direction (search for the first GOOD instead of first bad).
support_matrix.mechanism: the same good/bad/skip contract as `git bisect run`, so any test harness that already exits 0/1 works unchanged.

support_matrix.case: jj file search finds a pattern in the tree of an arbitrary revision WITHOUT checking it out
support_matrix.command:
jj file search --pattern variance            (at @)
jj file search --pattern variance -r 'base+2'  (before variance was added)
support_matrix.expectation: hit at @, empty at the earlier revision
support_matrix.observed: at @ -> stats.py; at base+2 (a revision predating the variance() commit) -> empty result
support_matrix.mechanism: unlike `git grep` (working tree / index) or ripgrep (working-tree files), jj searches the stored tree of any named revision directly — no checkout, no stash. This is the differentiator.

support_matrix.case: a colocated repo lets plain git see the jj commits verbatim
support_matrix.expectation: git shows the same commits jj created
support_matrix.observed: git log shows the jj commits (e.g. 2e5a1b9 = the xusp bug commit) with no translation step
support_matrix.mechanism: jj writes real git objects into a shared .git, so jj sits ON TOP of git rather than replacing it — teammates keep using git, you use jj. This lowers the adoption barrier.

support_matrix.case: the operation log makes a bisect's temporary checkouts fully undoable
support_matrix.command: jj op log ; jj op restore <op-id>
support_matrix.expectation: each bisection step is a recorded operation that can be rolled back in one command
support_matrix.observed: bisect prints, on completion, the exact `jj op restore <id>` to discard every temporary revision it created during the search
support_matrix.mechanism: every jj state change is an operation; `jj op restore` rewinds the whole repo to a prior operation, so a destructive-looking search is safe to run and trivially reversible.

freshness.bisect_run_introduced: 0.33.0 (2025-09-03) — NOT new in 0.43; the post says so explicitly and does not overstate the hook
freshness.identity: created by Martin von Zweigbergk (Google), written in Rust, uses the git on-disk format (real git commits), still 0.x (pre-1.0)
freshness.sourced_from: GitHub releases + changelog + docs.jj-vcs.dev + `jj --version` / `jj bisect --help`, cross-checked 2026-07-15

caveats.note: bisect assumes the bug is monotone — every descendant of the first bad commit is also bad. A bug that appears, disappears, and reappears (non-monotone) breaks that assumption and bisect can land on the wrong commit. The demo history is deliberately monotone.

jj file search is an early feature (0.41): its --help states glob matching only unless you ask for regex, no concurrent search, and no in-file match positions — it returns a FILE LIST, not line numbers. Do not expect grep-grade output yet.
jj is still 0.x. Behaviour and flags can change between minor releases; pin the version (0.43.x here) if you depend on exact output.

the change-id prefixes and git hashes in bisect-output.txt are run-specific — jj randomizes change-ids per repo. A re-run finds the same first-bad DESCRIPTION in the same number of steps, but with different id strings. See manifest.determinism.

explicitly_not_verified.note: bisect / file-search performance on a large, real repository was NOT measured. This is an 8-commit toy history; it says nothing about wall-clock cost at scale.
only Windows 11 was exercised (with the MAX_PATH workaround). The bisect logic is OS-independent, but no macOS/Linux run was captured here.
